title: Health & Emergencies
shortSummary: Describe symptoms, visit a pharmacy or clinic, and call for help in an emergency in Korean.
culturalNote: Korea has an excellent, affordable healthcare system, and pharmacies (약국, yakguk) are everywhere for minor ailments. For anything more serious, clinics (병원, byeongwon) are easy to access and often do not require an appointment. The key emergency numbers are 119 for fire and ambulance and 112 for police, both staffed and increasingly able to help in English in major cities. When describing symptoms you use 아파요 (apayo, it hurts) with the body part, as in 머리가 아파요 (meoriga apayo, my head hurts). Koreans value 건강 (geongang, health) highly, and there are many traditional remedies and foods believed to restore energy. In a real emergency the most important phrases are 도와주세요 (dowajuseyo, please help) and 구급차를 불러 주세요 (gugeupchareul bulleo juseyo, please call an ambulance). Staying polite even under stress is normal, and people will respond quickly and kindly to a clear request for help.

keyPhrases.english: I have to go to the hospital

grammar.explanation: To say a body part hurts, name it with the subject particle 이/가 and follow with 아파요 (apayo). 머리 (meori, head) becomes 머리가 아파요, and 배 (bae, stomach) becomes 배가 아파요.
grammar.commonMistake: Using the object particle 을/를 with 아파요; the body part is the subject, so use 이/가.

grammar.explanation: To say you must do something, attach 아야 해요 or 어야 해요 to the verb stem. 가다 (gada, to go) becomes 가야 해요 (gaya haeyo, I have to go).

examples.en: I have to go to the hospital.

grammar.commonMistake: Dropping 해요 at the end, which leaves the obligation meaning incomplete.

dialogue.setup: Nina feels unwell and asks a pharmacist for help.

lines.speaker: 니나
lines.english: Hello. I'm feeling a bit sick.

lines.speaker: 니나
lines.english: My head hurts and I have a fever.

lines.speaker: 약사
lines.english: Since when have you been sick?

lines.speaker: 니나
lines.english: Since yesterday. Do you have medicine?

lines.speaker: 약사
lines.native: 네, 이 약을 드세요. 안 나으면 병원에 가야 해요.
lines.transliteration: ne, i yageul deuseyo. an naeumyeon byeongwone gaya haeyo.
lines.english: Yes, take this medicine. If you don't get better, you should go to a clinic.

quiz.question: How do you say 'Please help!' in Korean?
quiz.explanation: 도와주세요 (dowajuseyo) is the urgent call for help.

quiz.question: Which sentence means 'My head hurts'?
quiz.explanation: 머리가 아파요 (meoriga apayo) means 'my head hurts'.

quiz.question: 병원에 가___ 해요. (I have to go)
quiz.explanation: The obligation pattern is stem + 아/어야 해요, so 가야 해요 means 'I have to go'.

quiz.question: What number do you call for an ambulance in Korea?
quiz.explanation: 119 is for fire and ambulance in Korea; 112 is for police.

quiz.question: Arrange: 주세요 / 구급차를 / 불러 (please call an ambulance)
quiz.explanation: Object, then verb, then the request: 구급차를 불러 주세요.
